query: If I had a wind asset in ERCOT West and a solar asset with the same capacity in CAISO SP15 zone in 2024, which one would make me the most merchant revenue assuming real time market prices only and no curtailment in both regions. Use revenue numbers in $/MWh for the comparison
final_answer:
## Results: ERCOT West Wind vs. CAISO SP15 Solar — 2024 Real-Time Merchant Revenue

### Headline Comparison ($/MWh)

| Metric | ERCOT West Wind | CAISO SP15 Solar | Winner |
|--------|----------------|------------------|--------|
| **Revenue per MWh** | **$19.87/MWh** | **$44.13/MWh** | **Solar (2.2× higher)** |
| Revenue per MW-year | $73,706/MW-yr | $82,053/MW-yr | Solar |
| Total generation (1 MW) | 3,709 MWh | 1,860 MWh | Wind |
| Capacity factor | 42.2% | 21.2% | Wind |
| Avg price during generation | $26.98/MWh | $38.27/MWh | Solar |

### Answer: **CAISO SP15 Solar wins on a $/MWh basis by a wide margin — $44.13/MWh vs. $19.87/MWh (2.2× higher).**

Even on a total $/MW-year basis, the solar asset in CAISO SP15 generates more merchant revenue ($82,053/MW-yr vs. $73,706/MW-yr), despite producing only **half** the energy of the wind asset.

---

### Key Drivers

1. **Price capture timing**: Solar in CAISO SP15 generates during daytime hours when prices are elevated (especially summer afternoons with high AC load). Wind in ERCOT West tends to blow more at night when prices are depressed. The average real-time price during solar generation hours was $38.27/MWh vs. $26.98/MWh during wind generation hours.

2. **CAISO SP15 price spikes**: The solar asset captured significant value during summer 2024 heat events. July solar revenue was $76.45/MWh and January was $80.24/MWh (driven by winter gas price spikes in CAISO).

3. **ERCOT West wind price cannibalization**: ERCOT West is heavily saturated with wind generation. When wind blows hardest (often at night), it drives prices down — a classic "cannibalization effect." Wind revenue per MWh was consistently low, ranging from $11–28/MWh across months.

4. **Overall price levels**: The time-weighted average real-time price was $31.00/MWh in CAISO SP15 vs. $27.41/MWh at ERCOT HB_WEST — but the generation-weighted spread was much wider ($38.27 vs. $26.98) because of the temporal correlation between generation and prices.

### Monthly Revenue per MWh ($/MWh)

| Month | Wind (ERCOT West) | Solar (CAISO SP15) |
|-------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Jan | $28.08 | $80.24 |
| Feb | $13.29 | $46.69 |
| Mar | $18.06 | $30.26 |
| Apr | $15.19 | $21.76 |
| May | $25.40 | $20.27 |
| Jun | $19.18 | $33.84 |
| Jul | $18.76 | $76.45 |
| Aug | $27.01 | $45.28 |
| Sep | $21.10 | $44.31 |
| Oct | $11.31 | $49.00 |
| Nov | $22.43 | $45.90 |
| Dec | $23.60 | $48.27 |

Solar beat wind in $/MWh in **every single month** of 2024 except May (where ERCOT West wind captured $25.40/MWh vs. solar's $20.27/MWh).

---

### Sources
- **Wind generation profile**: Renewables.ninja API, Vestas V90 2000 turbine at lat 32.5°N, lon -100.4°W (ERCOT West), 80m hub height, 2024 hourly data
- **Solar generation profile**: Renewables.ninja API, 1 kW PV system at lat 34.05°N, lon -118.24°W (CAISO SP15/Los Angeles), 35° tilt, 180° azimuth, 10% system loss, 2024 hourly data
- **ERCOT real-time prices**: Grid Status dataset `ercot_spp_real_time_15_min`, settlement point HB_WEST, resampled to hourly mean, 2024 full year
- **CAISO real-time prices**: Grid Status dataset `caiso_lmp_real_time_15_min`, trading hub TH_SP15_GEN-APND, resampled to hourly mean, 2024 full year

For CAISO SP15, a good location would be around Los Angeles/Southern CA: lat ~34.05, lon ~-118.24

tool_input.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load generation profiles
wind_df = pd.read_csv('/work/wind_profile_20260618_224845.csv')
solar_df = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260618_224848.csv')

# Load price data (two halves each)
ercot_p1 = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_spp_real_time_15_min_20260618_224957.csv')
ercot_p2 = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_spp_real_time_15_min_20260618_225122.csv')
caiso_p1 = pd.read_csv('/work/caiso_lmp_real_time_15_min_20260618_225059.csv')
caiso_p2 = pd.read_csv('/work/caiso_lmp_real_time_15_min_20260618_225111.csv')

# Combine price halves
ercot_prices = pd.concat([ercot_p1, ercot_p2], ignore_index=True)
caiso_prices = pd.concat([caiso_p1, caiso_p2], ignore_index=True)

print("Wind profile shape:", wind_df.shape)
print("Solar profile shape:", solar_df.shape)
print("ERCOT prices shape:", ercot_prices.shape)
print("CAISO prices shape:", caiso_prices.shape)
print()
print("Wind profile columns:", wind_df.columns.tolist())
print("Solar profile columns:", solar_df.columns.tolist())
print("ERCOT prices columns:", ercot_prices.columns.tolist())
print("CAISO prices columns:", caiso_prices.columns.tolist())
print()
print("Wind profile head:")
print(wind_df.head())
print()
print("ERCOT prices head:")
print(ercot_prices.head())
print()
print("CAISO prices head:")
print(caiso_prices.head())

steps.content: All 8,784 rows align perfectly. Now let me merge the data and calculate the revenue metrics.

tool_input.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load generation profiles
wind_df = pd.read_csv('/work/wind_profile_20260618_224845.csv')
solar_df = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260618_224848.csv')

# Load price data
ercot_p1 = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_spp_real_time_15_min_20260618_224957.csv')
ercot_p2 = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_spp_real_time_15_min_20260618_225122.csv')
caiso_p1 = pd.read_csv('/work/caiso_lmp_real_time_15_min_20260618_225059.csv')
caiso_p2 = pd.read_csv('/work/caiso_lmp_real_time_15_min_20260618_225111.csv')

# Combine price halves
ercot_prices = pd.concat([ercot_p1, ercot_p2], ignore_index=True)
caiso_prices = pd.concat([caiso_p1, caiso_p2], ignore_index=True)

# Parse timestamps - generation profiles are in local time (no timezone), prices in UTC
# The renewables.ninja profiles are in local time. Let's align by row index since both have 8784 rows for 2024 (leap year)
# The price data is hourly UTC starting 2024-01-01 00:00 UTC
# The generation profiles start at 2024-01-01 00:00 local time

# For a proper comparison, we need to align by UTC time.
# Wind profile is for ERCOT West (CST, UTC-6 in winter, UTC-5 in summer)
# Solar profile is for CAISO SP15 (PST, UTC-8 in winter, UTC-7 in summer)

# The renewables.ninja data is in local time. Let's convert to UTC.
# Actually, let's check: the wind profile starts at 2024-01-01 00:00 local (CST = UTC-6)
# So 2024-01-01 00:00 CST = 2024-01-01 06:00 UTC

# Let's create proper UTC timestamps for the generation profiles
# Wind: ERCOT West is in CST/CDT (UTC-6/UTC-5)
# Solar: CAISO SP15 is in PST/PDT (UTC-8/UTC-7)

# For simplicity, since both generation and price data have exactly 8784 hourly rows for 2024,
# and the price data is in UTC starting at 2024-01-01 00:00 UTC,
# we need to convert generation profiles to UTC.

# Let's use pandas with timezone handling
# Wind profile - local time is America/Chicago (CST/CDT)
wind_df['time'] = pd.to_datetime(wind_df['time'])
wind_df['time_utc'] = wind_df['time'].dt.tz_localize('America/Chicago').dt.tz_convert('UTC')

# Solar profile - local time is America/Los_Angeles (PST/PDT)
solar_df['time'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_df['time'])
solar_df['time_utc'] = solar_df['time'].dt.tz_localize('America/Los_Angeles').dt.tz_convert('UTC')

# Price data - parse UTC timestamps
ercot_prices['interval_start_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(ercot_prices['interval_start_utc'])
caiso_prices['interval_start_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(caiso_prices['interval_start_utc'])

# Merge wind with ERCOT prices
wind_merged = wind_df.merge(ercot_prices[['interval_start_utc', 'spp']], 
                            left_on='time_utc', right_on='interval_start_utc', how='inner')

# Merge solar with CAISO prices
solar_merged = solar_df.merge(caiso_prices[['interval_start_utc', 'lmp']], 
                              left_on='time_utc', right_on='interval_start_utc', how='inner')

print(f"Wind merged rows: {len(wind_merged)}")
print(f"Solar merged rows: {len(solar_merged)}")

# Calculate revenue
# electricity column is capacity factor (0-1) for 1 kW system = 1 MW system (normalized)
# For 1 MW capacity, generation in each hour = capacity_factor * 1 MW * 1 hour = capacity_factor MWh
# Revenue = generation (MWh) * price ($/MWh)

wind_merged['generation_mwh'] = wind_merged['electricity'] * 1  # 1 MW * 1 hour * capacity factor
wind_merged['revenue_usd'] = wind_merged['generation_mwh'] * wind_merged['spp']

solar_merged['generation_mwh'] = solar_merged['electricity'] * 1
solar_merged['revenue_usd'] = solar_merged['generation_mwh'] * solar_merged['lmp']

# Calculate metrics
wind_total_gen = wind_merged['generation_mwh'].sum()
wind_total_rev = wind_merged['revenue_usd'].sum()
wind_rev_per_mwh = wind_total_rev / wind_total_gen if wind_total_gen > 0 else 0
wind_rev_per_mw = wind_total_rev / 1  # per MW of capacity

solar_total_gen = solar_merged['generation_mwh'].sum()
solar_total_rev = solar_merged['revenue_usd'].sum()
solar_rev_per_mwh = solar_total_rev / solar_total_gen if solar_total_gen > 0 else 0
solar_rev_per_mw = solar_total_rev / 1  # per MW of capacity

print("\n" + "="*80)
print("COMPARISON: ERCOT West Wind vs CAISO SP15 Solar (2024, Real-Time Market)")
print("="*80)

print(f"\n--- ERCOT West Wind Asset (1 MW) ---")
print(f"Total generation:       {wind_total_gen:,.1f} MWh")
print(f"Capacity factor:        {wind_total_gen/8784*100:.1f}%")
print(f"Total revenue:          ${wind_total_rev:,.0f}")
print(f"Revenue per MWh:        ${wind_rev_per_mwh:.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Revenue per MW-year:    ${wind_rev_per_mw:,.0f}/MW-yr")

print(f"\n--- CAISO SP15 Solar Asset (1 MW) ---")
print(f"Total generation:       {solar_total_gen:,.1f} MWh")
print(f"Capacity factor:        {solar_total_gen/8784*100:.1f}%")
print(f"Total revenue:          ${solar_total_rev:,.0f}")
print(f"Revenue per MWh:        ${solar_rev_per_mwh:.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Revenue per MW-year:    ${solar_rev_per_mw:,.0f}/MW-yr")

print(f"\n--- Comparison ---")
print(f"Revenue per MWh difference:  ${solar_rev_per_mwh - wind_rev_per_mwh:.2f}/MWh (Solar - Wind)")
print(f"Revenue per MW difference:   ${solar_rev_per_mw - wind_rev_per_mw:,.0f}/MW-yr (Solar - Wind)")
print(f"Solar/Wind revenue per MWh ratio: {solar_rev_per_mwh/wind_rev_per_mwh:.2f}x")

# Also compute average prices during generation hours
wind_avg_price_gen = wind_merged.loc[wind_merged['generation_mwh'] > 0, 'spp'].mean()
solar_avg_price_gen = solar_merged.loc[solar_merged['generation_mwh'] > 0, 'lmp'].mean()
print(f"\nAvg price during wind generation hours:   ${wind_avg_price_gen:.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Avg price during solar generation hours:  ${solar_avg_price_gen:.2f}/MWh")

# Time-weighted average price (all hours)
print(f"\nTime-weighted avg price ERCOT West (all hours):  ${ercot_prices['spp'].mean():.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Time-weighted avg price CAISO SP15 (all hours):  ${caiso_prices['lmp'].mean():.2f}/MWh")

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load generation profiles
wind_df = pd.read_csv('/work/wind_profile_20260618_224845.csv')
solar_df = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260618_224848.csv')

# Load price data
ercot_p1 = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_spp_real_time_15_min_20260618_224957.csv')
ercot_p2 = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_spp_real_time_15_min_20260618_225122.csv')
caiso_p1 = pd.read_csv('/work/caiso_lmp_real_time_15_min_20260618_225059.csv')
caiso_p2 = pd.read_csv('/work/caiso_lmp_real_time_15_min_20260618_225111.csv')

# Combine price halves
ercot_prices = pd.concat([ercot_p1, ercot_p2], ignore_index=True)
caiso_prices = pd.concat([caiso_p1, caiso_p2], ignore_index=True)

# Parse timestamps with DST handling
wind_df['time'] = pd.to_datetime(wind_df['time'])
wind_df['time_utc'] = wind_df['time'].dt.tz_localize('America/Chicago', nonexistent='shift_forward', ambiguous='NaT').dt.tz_convert('UTC')

solar_df['time'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_df['time'])
solar_df['time_utc'] = solar_df['time'].dt.tz_localize('America/Los_Angeles', nonexistent='shift_forward', ambiguous='NaT').dt.tz_convert('UTC')

# Price data - parse UTC timestamps
ercot_prices['interval_start_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(ercot_prices['interval_start_utc'], utc=True)
caiso_prices['interval_start_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(caiso_prices['interval_start_utc'], utc=True)

# Drop NaT rows from ambiguous DST times
wind_df = wind_df.dropna(subset=['time_utc'])
solar_df = solar_df.dropna(subset=['time_utc'])

# Merge wind with ERCOT prices
wind_merged = wind_df.merge(ercot_prices[['interval_start_utc', 'spp']], 
                            left_on='time_utc', right_on='interval_start_utc', how='inner')

# Merge solar with CAISO prices
solar_merged = solar_df.merge(caiso_prices[['interval_start_utc', 'lmp']], 
                              left_on='time_utc', right_on='interval_start_utc', how='inner')

print(f"Wind merged rows: {len(wind_merged)}")
print(f"Solar merged rows: {len(solar_merged)}")

# Calculate revenue
# electricity column is capacity factor (0-1) for 1 MW system
# For 1 MW capacity, generation in each hour = capacity_factor * 1 MW * 1 hour = capacity_factor MWh
wind_merged['generation_mwh'] = wind_merged['electricity'] * 1
wind_merged['revenue_usd'] = wind_merged['generation_mwh'] * wind_merged['spp']

solar_merged['generation_mwh'] = solar_merged['electricity'] * 1
solar_merged['revenue_usd'] = solar_merged['generation_mwh'] * solar_merged['lmp']

# Calculate metrics
wind_total_gen = wind_merged['generation_mwh'].sum()
wind_total_rev = wind_merged['revenue_usd'].sum()
wind_rev_per_mwh = wind_total_rev / wind_total_gen if wind_total_gen > 0 else 0
wind_rev_per_mw = wind_total_rev / 1

solar_total_gen = solar_merged['generation_mwh'].sum()
solar_total_rev = solar_merged['revenue_usd'].sum()
solar_rev_per_mwh = solar_total_rev / solar_total_gen if solar_total_gen > 0 else 0
solar_rev_per_mw = solar_total_rev / 1

print("\n" + "="*80)
print("COMPARISON: ERCOT West Wind vs CAISO SP15 Solar (2024, Real-Time Market)")
print("="*80)

print(f"\n--- ERCOT West Wind Asset (1 MW) ---")
print(f"Total generation:       {wind_total_gen:,.1f} MWh")
print(f"Capacity factor:        {wind_total_gen/8784*100:.1f}%")
print(f"Total revenue:          ${wind_total_rev:,.0f}")
print(f"Revenue per MWh:        ${wind_rev_per_mwh:.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Revenue per MW-year:    ${wind_rev_per_mw:,.0f}/MW-yr")

print(f"\n--- CAISO SP15 Solar Asset (1 MW) ---")
print(f"Total generation:       {solar_total_gen:,.1f} MWh")
print(f"Capacity factor:        {solar_total_gen/8784*100:.1f}%")
print(f"Total revenue:          ${solar_total_rev:,.0f}")
print(f"Revenue per MWh:        ${solar_rev_per_mwh:.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Revenue per MW-year:    ${solar_rev_per_mw:,.0f}/MW-yr")

print(f"\n--- Comparison ---")
print(f"Revenue per MWh difference:  ${solar_rev_per_mwh - wind_rev_per_mwh:.2f}/MWh (Solar - Wind)")
print(f"Revenue per MW difference:   ${solar_rev_per_mw - wind_rev_per_mw:,.0f}/MW-yr (Solar - Wind)")
print(f"Solar/Wind revenue per MWh ratio: {solar_rev_per_mwh/wind_rev_per_mwh:.2f}x")

# Also compute average prices during generation hours
wind_avg_price_gen = wind_merged.loc[wind_merged['generation_mwh'] > 0, 'spp'].mean()
solar_avg_price_gen = solar_merged.loc[solar_merged['generation_mwh'] > 0, 'lmp'].mean()
print(f"\nAvg price during wind generation hours:   ${wind_avg_price_gen:.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Avg price during solar generation hours:  ${solar_avg_price_gen:.2f}/MWh")

# Time-weighted average price (all hours)
print(f"\nTime-weighted avg price ERCOT West (all hours):  ${ercot_prices['spp'].mean():.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Time-weighted avg price CAISO SP15 (all hours):  ${caiso_prices['lmp'].mean():.2f}/MWh")

# Monthly breakdown
wind_merged['month'] = wind_merged['time_utc'].dt.month
solar_merged['month'] = solar_merged['time_utc'].dt.month

print("\n--- Monthly Revenue per MWh ($/MWh) ---")
print(f"{'Month':<8} {'Wind':>10} {'Solar':>10}")
for m in range(1, 13):
    w_data = wind_merged[wind_merged['month'] == m]
    s_data = solar_merged[solar_merged['month'] == m]
    w_rpm = w_data['revenue_usd'].sum() / w_data['generation_mwh'].sum() if w_data['generation_mwh'].sum() > 0 else 0
    s_rpm = s_data['revenue_usd'].sum() / s_data['generation_mwh'].sum() if s_data['generation_mwh'].sum() > 0 else 0
    print(f"{m:<8} ${w_rpm:>9.2f} ${s_rpm:>9.2f}")

steps.content:
## Results: ERCOT West Wind vs. CAISO SP15 Solar — 2024 Real-Time Merchant Revenue

### Headline Comparison ($/MWh)

| Metric | ERCOT West Wind | CAISO SP15 Solar | Winner |
|--------|----------------|------------------|--------|
| **Revenue per MWh** | **$19.87/MWh** | **$44.13/MWh** | **Solar (2.2× higher)** |
| Revenue per MW-year | $73,706/MW-yr | $82,053/MW-yr | Solar |
| Total generation (1 MW) | 3,709 MWh | 1,860 MWh | Wind |
| Capacity factor | 42.2% | 21.2% | Wind |
| Avg price during generation | $26.98/MWh | $38.27/MWh | Solar |

### Answer: **CAISO SP15 Solar wins on a $/MWh basis by a wide margin — $44.13/MWh vs. $19.87/MWh (2.2× higher).**

Even on a total $/MW-year basis, the solar asset in CAISO SP15 generates more merchant revenue ($82,053/MW-yr vs. $73,706/MW-yr), despite producing only **half** the energy of the wind asset.

---

### Key Drivers

1. **Price capture timing**: Solar in CAISO SP15 generates during daytime hours when prices are elevated (especially summer afternoons with high AC load). Wind in ERCOT West tends to blow more at night when prices are depressed. The average real-time price during solar generation hours was $38.27/MWh vs. $26.98/MWh during wind generation hours.

2. **CAISO SP15 price spikes**: The solar asset captured significant value during summer 2024 heat events. July solar revenue was $76.45/MWh and January was $80.24/MWh (driven by winter gas price spikes in CAISO).

3. **ERCOT West wind price cannibalization**: ERCOT West is heavily saturated with wind generation. When wind blows hardest (often at night), it drives prices down — a classic "cannibalization effect." Wind revenue per MWh was consistently low, ranging from $11–28/MWh across months.

4. **Overall price levels**: The time-weighted average real-time price was $31.00/MWh in CAISO SP15 vs. $27.41/MWh at ERCOT HB_WEST — but the generation-weighted spread was much wider ($38.27 vs. $26.98) because of the temporal correlation between generation and prices.

### Monthly Revenue per MWh ($/MWh)

| Month | Wind (ERCOT West) | Solar (CAISO SP15) |
|-------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Jan | $28.08 | $80.24 |
| Feb | $13.29 | $46.69 |
| Mar | $18.06 | $30.26 |
| Apr | $15.19 | $21.76 |
| May | $25.40 | $20.27 |
| Jun | $19.18 | $33.84 |
| Jul | $18.76 | $76.45 |
| Aug | $27.01 | $45.28 |
| Sep | $21.10 | $44.31 |
| Oct | $11.31 | $49.00 |
| Nov | $22.43 | $45.90 |
| Dec | $23.60 | $48.27 |

Solar beat wind in $/MWh in **every single month** of 2024 except May (where ERCOT West wind captured $25.40/MWh vs. solar's $20.27/MWh).

---

### Sources
- **Wind generation profile**: Renewables.ninja API, Vestas V90 2000 turbine at lat 32.5°N, lon -100.4°W (ERCOT West), 80m hub height, 2024 hourly data
- **Solar generation profile**: Renewables.ninja API, 1 kW PV system at lat 34.05°N, lon -118.24°W (CAISO SP15/Los Angeles), 35° tilt, 180° azimuth, 10% system loss, 2024 hourly data
- **ERCOT real-time prices**: Grid Status dataset `ercot_spp_real_time_15_min`, settlement point HB_WEST, resampled to hourly mean, 2024 full year
- **CAISO real-time prices**: Grid Status dataset `caiso_lmp_real_time_15_min`, trading hub TH_SP15_GEN-APND, resampled to hourly mean, 2024 full year
steps.reasoning:
Now I have all the results. Let me summarize the findings.

Key results:
- ERCOT West Wind: $19.87/MWh revenue, $73,706/MW-yr
- CAISO SP15 Solar: $44.13/MWh revenue, $82,053/MW-yr

The solar asset in CAISO SP15 generates more total merchant revenue per MW of capacity ($82,053 vs $73,706), and significantly more revenue per MWh ($44.13 vs $19.87).

The question asks which would make the most merchant revenue and to use $/MWh for the comparison. The CAISO SP15 solar asset wins on a $/MWh basis at $44.13/MWh vs $19.87/MWh for ERCOT West wind.

Let me also note the key drivers:
1. CAISO SP15 has higher overall prices ($31.00/MWh time-weighted avg vs $27.41/MWh for ERCOT West)
2. Solar in CAISO captures better prices during daytime hours, especially in summer months when prices spike
3. Wind in ERCOT West has a much higher capacity factor (42.2% vs 21.2%) but captures lower prices, especially because wind tends to blow more at night when prices are low
4. The solar asset also wins on total revenue per MW despite generating only half the energy, because of the much higher price capture

Let me present this clearly.
